research_series.series_name: Postprandial State, Fasting, Ketones, Fatigue, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Intermittent fasting and time-restricted eating improve metabolic health and may benefit cognitive function in older adults

mediator.name: glucose_and_ketone_bodies
mediator.pathway: energy metabolism shift from glucose to ketone utilization during fasting periods
mediator.mechanism: improved insulin sensitivity, increased beta-hydroxybutyrate production, reduced inflammation

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Intermittent fasting or time-restricted eating leads to metabolic changes (lower glucose, higher ketones) that are associated with improved or stable cognitive function and mental health in older adults.

hypotheses.reason: Systematic reviews and RCTs show positive or neutral effects on cognition and mood in older adults practicing intermittent fasting or TRE.

evidence.summary: Systematic reviews and RCTs indicate that intermittent fasting and TRE improve metabolic markers including glucose, insulin, and ketones and may benefit cognitive function and mental health in older adults. Effects on cognition are generally positive or neutral; more research is needed for definitive conclusions.

key_sources.title: Effect of time-restricted eating and intermittent fasting on cognitive function and mental health in older adults: A systematic review
key_sources.relevant_finding: TRE and intermittent fasting may have a positive impact on cognitive function and mental health in older adults.
key_sources.limitations: Heterogeneity of interventions; need for more high-quality trials.

key_sources.title: Time-restricted feeding and cognitive function in sedentary and physically active elderly individuals: Ramadan diurnal intermittent fasting as a model
key_sources.relevant_finding: Physically active elderly participants improved in executive function, attention, inhibition, and associative memory during Ramadan intermittent fasting; sedentary participants showed decreased associative learning.
key_sources.limitations: Subjective sleep measures; cultural adaptation of tests.

limitations: Potential confounding by physical activity or sleep changes
alternative_explanations: Social or cultural factors during religious fasts
Diet composition differences unrelated to timing

patterns.pattern_name: Beta-hydroxybutyrate is positively associated with general cognitive performance in humans

mediator.name: beta_hydroxybutyrate
mediator.pathway: alternative brain energy substrate during low glucose availability
mediator.mechanism: supports neuronal energy metabolism; may reduce neuroinflammation

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Higher BHB levels are associated with better general cognitive performance; genetic evidence may support a protective association against cognitive decline.

hypotheses.reason: Observational regression and Mendelian randomization studies report that BHB predicts better cognition and lower AD risk.

evidence.summary: Observational and Mendelian randomization studies report that higher BHB predicts better cognition and lower AD risk. This is supported indirectly by meta-analyses of ketogenic diet interventions in AD patients, but general-population causal inference remains limited.

key_sources.title: Triangulating evidence from observational and Mendelian randomization studies of ketone bodies for cognitive performance
key_sources.relevant_finding: BHB positively associated with general cognitive function; MR supports protective effect against AD.

key_sources.title: Effects of ketogenic diet on cognitive function of patients with Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: Ketogenic diet improves MMSE or ADAS-Cog scores in AD patients and increases BHB levels.

limitations: Causality not proven for short-term interventions
Most direct evidence comes from AD populations
Potential confounding by overall metabolic health
alternative_explanations: Reverse causality: healthier individuals may have higher BHB
Other dietary or lifestyle factors correlated with BHB

patterns.pattern_name: Short-term fasting does not meaningfully impair cognitive performance in healthy adults, but longer fasts may cause modest reductions

mediator.name: glucose_availability
mediator.mechanism: lowered blood glucose triggers compensatory ketogenesis

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Short-term fasting up to about 12 hours does not impair cognition; longer fasts may cause small decrements.

hypotheses.reason: Meta-analysis shows no meaningful difference between fasted and satiated participants overall.

evidence.summary: A meta-analysis of more than 3000 participants reports no meaningful difference between fasted and satiated groups for most cognitive domains.

key_sources.title: Acute effects of fasting on cognitive performance: A systematic review and meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: No meaningful difference between fasted and satiated participants overall. Modest reductions may appear for longer fasts or younger participants.
key_sources.limitations: Some heterogeneity for long fasts and young subjects.

limitations: Not all cognitive domains tested equally
Longer fasts greater than 24 hours are less studied

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: No strong human evidence links postprandial glucose spikes directly to brain fog or acute cognitive fatigue.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: No included study performed formal mediation analysis or demonstrated an acute causal chain from postprandial hyperglycemia to subjective brain fog or cognitive fatigue.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: The primary circulating ketone body produced during ketosis; alternative brain fuel when glucose is low.
Dietary pattern restricting food intake to specific daily windows such as 8 to 10 hours.

source_verification_queue.reason: DOI or URL missing; verify bibliographic metadata before adding to source registry.

source_verification_queue.source_title: Triangulating evidence from observational and Mendelian randomization studies of ketone bodies for cognitive performance
source_verification_queue.reason: DOI or URL missing; verify MR assumptions, exposure definition, and outcome dataset.

source_verification_queue.source_title: Acute effects of fasting on cognitive performance: A systematic review and meta-analysis
source_verification_queue.reason: Year 2025 and DOI/URL missing; verify publication status and exact conclusions.

source_verification_queue.source_title: Effects of ketogenic diet on cognitive function of patients with Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source_verification_queue.reason: DOI or URL missing; verify included population and whether findings generalize beyond AD.

source_verification_queue.source_title: Time-restricted feeding and cognitive function in sedentary and physically active elderly individuals
source_verification_queue.reason: DOI or URL missing; verify study design, fasting model, sleep confounding, and test battery.

research_gaps: Lack of direct mediation analyses linking postprandial glycemic excursions to acute changes in attention, fatigue, or mood.
Inadequate mechanistic human studies connecting gut hormones such as ghrelin and leptin to motivation or cognition after meal timing manipulations.
Heterogeneity in outcome measures for subjective fatigue and brain fog limits synthesis.

most_supported_patterns: Intermittent fasting and TRE improve metabolic markers and may benefit cognition or mood in older adults.
Higher beta-hydroxybutyrate is associated with better general cognition.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Beta-hydroxybutyrate as a node linking metabolic state to neurobehavioral outcomes.
Time-restricted eating as an actionable physiological input.
patterns_requiring_caution: Direct causal claims about postprandial glucose spikes causing brain fog or fatigue remain unproven.
Short-term fasting generally does not impair cognition, but individual variability exists.
recommended_next_queries: Are acute postprandial glycemic excursions causally linked to subjective brain fog or measured attention/fatigue deficits?
Do ghrelin/leptin fluctuations after meal skipping mediate changes in motivation/executive function?
summary.overall_assessment: Human evidence supports beneficial associations between intermittent fasting, TRE, metabolic switching, higher BHB, and some aspects of cognition or mood, especially in older or metabolically impaired populations. Direct causal chains from postprandial glycemic spikes to acute neurobehavioral impairment remain speculative. Mechanistic mediation analyses are rare. Most findings are associative rather than strictly causal.
